title: The Second Sunday after Christmas
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: January 2, 2022

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Second Sunday after Christmas Day, occurring within the Christmas Season (extending through January 5, 2022, until the Feast of the Epiphany). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would typically include Jeremiah 31:7-14 (or Sirach 24:1-12), Psalm 147:12-20, Ephesians 1:3-14, and John 1:1-18, with the Gospel focusing on the Prologue of John emphasizing the Word made flesh. This places the congregation in the liturgical season of contemplating the Incarnation during the octave period when churches transition from Christmas focus toward Epiphany themes of manifestation.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the aftermath of the 78th General Convention (held in Baltimore in July 2021), which had passed significant resolutions on racial justice and LGBTQ+ inclusion that were still being digested by dioceses. The Diocese of Colorado (which includes Saint John's Cathedral in Denver) was in the tenure of Bishop Kimberly Lucas, elected in 2019, who was focusing on pandemic recovery and renewal initiatives. No major denominational crisis or bishop election was occurring at this specific moment, though the church remained engaged with ongoing conversations about vaccine mandates for clergy and staff that had dominated late 2021.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing unusually mild weather for early January 2022, with temperatures near 50°F in the first week of the year, quite atypical for the Front Range winter season. The city was still in partial recovery mode from the significant winter storm of December 26-27, 2021, which had caused power outages and travel disruptions across the region just days before this sermon. Capitol Hill and downtown Denver neighborhoods were busy with New Year's Day recovery and the return of residents to normal routines after the holiday period.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the surge of COVID-19 cases driven by the Omicron variant, which was overwhelming hospitals nationwide in the first week of January 2022, with case counts reaching record levels unseen even during previous pandemic waves. The January 6 Committee was actively investigating the Capitol riot of January 6, 2021, exactly one year prior, with hearings and subpoenas making headlines as the 2022 midterm election cycle approached. The NFL playoffs were beginning, with the Denver Broncos preparing for their playoff run, a story of significant local interest (the team finished the regular season 7-10 and would miss the playoffs, though this may not have been fully settled on January 2).

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Ukraine was experiencing heightened tensions as Russia had amassed over 100,000 troops on its border, with Western intelligence agencies warning of potential invasion plans in the coming months (the full invasion would occur February 24, 2022). The WHO and international health agencies were tracking the spread of the Omicron variant globally, with many countries implementing new restrictions and travel bans in early January 2022. China was preparing for the Winter Olympics to be held in Beijing in February 2022, with security and human rights concerns dominating international coverage as the Games approached.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood in early January 2022 was marked by exhaustion and anxiety regarding the pandemic, particularly as Omicron's rapid spread suggested the crisis would extend further into 2022 rather than ending as many had hoped in late 2021. There was political polarization around pandemic responses, vaccine mandates, and school closures, with these becoming flash points in communities nationwide. The cultural moment was one of hesitant return to normal—with many organizations reopening after holiday closures—coupled with genuine uncertainty about whether gatherings and in-person worship could safely continue, creating an undertone of caution even as people attempted to resume routines.
